faculty.name: Philipp Hennig
faculty.title: Professor for the Methods of Machine Learning
faculty.summary: Founder of probabilistic numerics: treating numerical computation — linear algebra, optimization, ODE/PDE solving — as probabilistic inference, so simulations return calibrated uncertainty. Recent work includes probabilistic ODE filters and their application to dynamical-systems learning, Laplace approximations for deep networks, and computation-aware Gaussian processes. Co-author of the Probabilistic Numerics textbook. Directly relevant to anyone blending scientific computing with machine learning.

faculty.name: Jakob Macke
faculty.title: Professor of Machine Learning in Science
faculty.summary: Develops simulation-based (likelihood-free) inference: neural density estimators that turn mechanistic simulators into statistically rigorous tools for parameter inference, with the widely used sbi software package. Applications span neuroscience, physics, and increasingly geoscience-style inverse problems where simulators are expensive and likelihoods intractable. Recent work improves amortized inference, model criticism, and scalability of SBI. A natural fit for science-oriented ML students.

faculty.name: Ulrike von Luxburg
faculty.title: Professor of Theory of Machine Learning
faculty.summary: Works on the theoretical foundations of machine learning: statistical guarantees for clustering and graph-based methods, ordinal embeddings and comparison-based learning, and recently the theory and critique of explainable ML. Known for the classic spectral-clustering tutorial and for rigorous analyses of when popular ML heuristics can and cannot be trusted. Suits students who want mathematically grounded ML alongside applied coursework.

requirements.fee_display: $100 (discounted to $80 if submitted before the Nov 18, 2026 early deadline)
requirements.english: TOEFL (preferred), IELTS, or Duolingo required for international applicants whose native language is not English; the department does not issue waivers for non-native English speakers.
requirements.admission_model_note: Admission is decided by a departmental faculty committee, not by an individual advisor. Applicants indicate research interests but are not expected to have an advisor lined up; official advisor matching occurs after admission, aided by a two-week introductory orientation.

funding.note: The department gives all admitted PhD students comprehensive support (stipend, tuition, university fees, and health insurance) while in good academic standing; funding is the same for domestic and international students across the roughly five-year program.

requirements.contact_note: Applicants are not expected to contact or secure a faculty advisor before applying, since advisor matching happens after admission.

faculty.name: Guy Blelloch
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Blelloch works on the theory and practice of parallel computing, designing work-efficient, provably fast shared-memory parallel algorithms and data structures. Recent efforts include batch-parallel and concurrent data structures, deterministic parallelism, and high-performance libraries such as ParlayLib, PAM, and the GBBS graph benchmark suite. His research bridges parallel algorithm theory with practical engineering, delivering scalable methods for sorting, graph analytics, and geometric problems on modern multicore machines.

faculty.name: Ryan O'Donnell
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: O'Donnell is a computational complexity theorist known for the analysis of Boolean functions, a viewpoint he applies to hardness of approximation, learning theory, and property testing. Recent work spans quantum computing, including near-optimal algorithms and lower bounds for quantum state tomography and learning quantum states, as well as spectral graph theory and pseudorandomness. He is also known for widely used educational materials on complexity and analysis of Boolean functions.

faculty.name: Andrew Pavlo
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Pavlo builds database management systems, with an emphasis on autonomous, self-driving databases that use machine learning to tune, optimize, and manage themselves. His group produced the NoisePage self-driving DBMS and the OtterTune automatic configuration service, and studies in-memory storage, transaction processing, and modern hardware. Recent work explores applying learned models and, increasingly, large language models to database administration and query optimization, aiming to remove humans from the operational loop.

faculty.name: Justine Sherry
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Sherry researches computer networking and networked systems, focusing on middleboxes, network functions, and the performance and correctness of data-plane infrastructure. Recent work covers congestion control analysis and fairness, high-speed packet processing, hardware-accelerated and programmable network functions, and verification of network behavior. She combines systems building with rigorous measurement and theory to make in-network services faster, more predictable, and easier to reason about at scale.

faculty.name: Mor Harchol-Balter
faculty.title: Dr. Bruce J. Nelson Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Harchol-Balter applies queueing theory and stochastic modeling to the design of computer systems, deriving scheduling and resource-allocation policies that reduce latency in servers, data centers, and cloud platforms. Recent work includes new analyses of size-based and multiserver scheduling (such as the SOAP framework), optimal load balancing, and performance of large-scale parallel and cloud systems. Her research pairs provable theoretical results with practical policies for real deployments, and she authored a widely used textbook on performance modeling.

faculty.name: Zico Kolter
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Kolter studies deep learning with an emphasis on robustness, safety, and structure in models. His group pioneered certified adversarial defenses, differentiable optimization layers (OptNet), and deep equilibrium models, and more recently developed automated adversarial attacks that jailbreak large language models. Current work centers on understanding and guaranteeing the reliability of modern AI systems, spanning robustness certification, model interpretability, and LLM alignment and safety. He is a leading voice in trustworthy machine learning.

faculty.name: Tianqi Chen
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Chen builds systems that make machine learning faster, cheaper, and more portable across hardware. He is the creator of widely adopted open-source tools including XGBoost, the TVM deep-learning compiler, and MXNet, and more recently MLC-LLM for running large language models efficiently on diverse devices. His research spans automated tensor-program optimization, compiler techniques for deep learning, and end-to-end machine-learning compilation, bridging algorithms, systems, and hardware for scalable model training and deployment.

faculty.name: Bryan Parno
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Parno works at the intersection of security, systems, and formal methods, building software whose security and correctness are proven mathematically. His group produced verified cryptographic libraries (such as EverCrypt and HACL*/Vale) and machine-checked distributed and systems software (including IronFleet), pushing full-functional-correctness verification to real, high-performance code. Recent work advances proof automation, verified concurrency, and verified cryptographic protocols, aiming to make provably secure systems practical rather than merely aspirational.

faculty.name: Tuomas Sandholm
faculty.title: Angel Jordan University Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Sandholm develops algorithms for solving large imperfect-information games and for market and mechanism design. His group built the poker AIs Libratus and Pluribus, the first to defeat top human professionals in no-limit Texas hold'em, and its techniques now extend to negotiation, cybersecurity, and strategic reasoning. He also pioneered algorithms for kidney-exchange matching used nationally and for combinatorial auctions and optimization, and he founded several companies commercializing this work.

faculty.name: Keenan Crane
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Crane works in computer graphics and discrete differential geometry, developing algorithms that treat geometric problems through elegant, provably robust mathematics. His group created the heat method and vector heat method for computing distances on surfaces, and recent grid-free Monte Carlo geometry-processing techniques (walk-on-spheres and its extensions) that solve partial differential equations on complex geometry without meshing. His work spans surface parameterization, shape analysis, and simulation, and he is known for exceptionally clear teaching of the underlying mathematics.

faculty.name: Umut Acar
faculty.title: Professor, Computer Science Department
faculty.summary: Acar develops provably efficient parallel functional programming. His MPL compiler for Standard ML brings automatic memory and parallelism management to functional languages, earning a POPL 2024 distinguished paper, and his ongoing work extends automatic parallelism management and disentanglement-based memory management. He also pioneered self-adjusting and incremental computation, where programs respond efficiently to changing data, and recently branched into local quantum circuit optimization (best paper, QCE 2025). His goal is to raise the abstraction level for reasoning about performance while preserving safety.

faculty.name: David Andersen
faculty.title: Professor, Computer Science Department
faculty.summary: Andersen builds efficient networked and distributed systems, focusing on how software and data structures should respond to the end of Moore's law. His FAWN project pioneered energy-efficient cluster architectures, and his group explores kernel-bypass networking to accelerate packet forwarding and high-performance key-value storage. Recent efforts target memory- and power-efficient computing, robust distributed systems, and machine-learning systems. His work has won SOSP best-paper and CMU's Newell Award; he is also founder and CTO of BrdgAI and a former visiting scientist with Google Brain.

faculty.name: Nathan Beckmann
faculty.title: Associate Professor, Computer Science Department
faculty.summary: Beckmann leads the Computer Organization Research Group (CORGi), designing architectures and systems that minimize data movement, the dominant cost in modern computing. His work spans smart caching at datacenter scale, optimal cache replacement with variable object sizes, and reconfigurable cache hierarchies, combining rigorous theory with practical prototypes. An NSF CAREER awardee, he studies energy-efficient memory systems and near-data and spatial computing, aiming to make accessing data faster and cheaper across the stack from chips to distributed caches.

faculty.name: Maria-Florina Balcan
faculty.title: Cadence Design Systems Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Balcan develops foundations for modern machine learning and algorithm design. Her influential program on data-driven algorithm design gives provable guarantees for tuning algorithms to problem distributions, spanning learning theory, optimization, and algorithmic game theory. Recent work analyzes revenue-maximizing mechanisms, learning decision-sufficient representations for optimization, and the dynamics of multi-agent systems adapting under limited information. A Simons Investigator, Sloan Fellow, and ACM Grace Murray Hopper Award recipient, she has co-chaired COLT, ICML, and NeurIPS.

faculty.name: Vincent Conitzer
faculty.title: Professor, Computer Science Department
faculty.summary: Conitzer directs CMU's Foundations of Cooperative AI Lab, working at the intersection of AI, game theory, and ethics. His research covers mechanism design, computational social choice, and how to determine the objectives AI systems should pursue, increasingly applying social choice and game theory to AI alignment and cooperative multi-agent settings. He also holds an appointment at Oxford's Institute for Ethics in AI. Honors include the IJCAI Computers and Thought Award, a PECASE, and a Guggenheim Fellowship.

faculty.name: David Woodruff
faculty.title: Professor, Computer Science Department
faculty.summary: Woodruff designs algorithms for big data, including foundational streaming and sketching techniques and randomized numerical linear algebra. Recent work develops adversarially robust streaming algorithms for insertion-deletion and turnstile streams, Lp sampling in distributed streams, and high-dimensional geometric streaming for nearly low-rank data. A Simons Investigator and Presburger Award winner, his count-distinct and subspace-embedding results underpin modern data-analysis systems, earning best-paper awards at STOC and PODS and a STOC Test of Time Award.

faculty.name: Elaine Shi
faculty.title: Professor of Computer Science and Electrical & Computer Engineering
faculty.summary: Shi works on cryptography, algorithms, game theory, and blockchain foundations. Her Oblivious RAM and differentially private algorithms have been deployed by Signal, Meta, and Google, and her recent research advances secure multi-party computation, oblivious data structures, and the theoretical foundations of consensus and mechanism design. A Sloan, Packard, ACM, and IACR Fellow, she is part of CMU's CyLab, co-founded Oblivious Labs, and builds practical privacy-preserving systems grounded in rigorous cryptographic guarantees.

faculty.name: Graham Neubig
faculty.title: Associate Professor, Language Technologies Institute
faculty.summary: Neubig leads NeuLab, advancing natural language processing and large language models. Recent work centers on language agents that act in software environments, including tools for autonomous software engineering and rigorous agent evaluation, alongside multilingual NLP, machine translation, and model interpretability. He co-created widely used open datasets, benchmarks, course materials, and open agent frameworks. He is actively recruiting students on topics related to LLMs, agents, and NLP evaluation, and champions accessible, openly published NLP research.

faculty.name: Deepak Pathak
faculty.title: Assistant Professor, Robotics Institute
faculty.summary: Pathak works at the intersection of computer vision, machine learning, and robotics, building agents that generalize across bodies and environments from raw sensory data. His group is known for curiosity-driven exploration and legged-locomotion controllers achieving extreme parkour with a single neural network, plus in-context adaptation letting one policy control entirely new robot bodies. He co-founded and leads Skild AI, developing a general-purpose robot brain that transfers across diverse hardware. He also holds an affiliate appointment in the Machine Learning Department.

faculty.name: Deva Ramanan
faculty.title: Professor, Robotics Institute
faculty.summary: Ramanan directs the CMU Argo AI Center for Autonomous Vehicle Research, studying visual recognition and understanding people and scenes from images and video. His work spans object detection, tracking, 3D perception, and long-tail recognition for safety-critical autonomy, with recent publications at CVPR, ICCV, and NeurIPS 2025. A former CVPR program chair and PAMI associate editor, he bridges foundational vision research with real-world perception for self-driving vehicles and other embodied systems.

faculty.name: Aditi Raghunathan
faculty.title: Assistant Professor, Computer Science Department
faculty.summary: Raghunathan builds machine learning that is reliable and robust, blending theory with practice. Her research addresses robustness to distribution shift and adversarial examples, the limits and reliability of foundation models, memorization in large language models, and AI safety, drawing on statistics, optimization, and algorithms. A Schmidt Sciences AI2050 Fellow whose Stanford thesis won the Arthur Samuel Best Thesis Award, she develops principled methods and guarantees for trustworthy real-world deployment of deep learning, with an affiliate appointment in the Machine Learning Department.

faculty.name: Chris Harrison
faculty.title: Associate Professor, Human-Computer Interaction Institute
faculty.summary: Harrison directs the Future Interfaces Group, inventing novel sensing technologies and interaction techniques for richer human-computer interaction. His work spans smart environments, wearable and on-body input, and augmented reality, turning everyday surfaces and devices into interactive interfaces. A Packard Fellow named to Forbes 30-under-30 and MIT Technology Review's TR35, he co-founded Qeexo, whose touch-sensing machine learning shipped on hundreds of millions of devices before its 2023 acquisition by TDK.

requirements.english: TOEFL 93 iBT (min 22 Writing and Speaking) or IELTS 7.0 overall (6.5 per band); waived if the admitting degree was completed in English.
requirements.admission_model_note: Applications are reviewed holistically by faculty with no rotations and no need to pre-arrange a supervisor; applicants name faculty of interest in their statement of purpose, and formal supervisor or research-group matching occurs after admission based on stated research interests.

funding.note: All admitted PhD students receive a guaranteed funding package (fellowships plus RA/TA) covering tuition and living costs: 44 months for students with an MSc from the department, 48 months with an MSc from elsewhere, and 60 months for the Direct-Entry PhD.

requirements.contact_note: The department explicitly advises applicants not to secure a supervisor before applying and instead to name faculty of interest in the statement of purpose.

faculty.name: Jimmy Ba
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Ba co-created the widely used Adam optimizer and works on efficient deep learning, optimization, and reasoning in large neural networks. His recent research explores in-context learning, large language model agents, and reinforcement learning for foundation models. From 2023 to early 2026 he was a core researcher at xAI helping build the Grok models before returning to focus on his Toronto group. His long-term goal is building general problem-solving machines with human-like efficiency and adaptability.

faculty.name: David Duvenaud
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Duvenaud, a Canada Research Chair in generative models and Vector Institute founding member, is known for Neural ODEs and automatic chemical design with generative models. His recent focus has shifted toward AI safety and governance, including sabotage and alignment evaluations of advanced systems, following a sabbatical on Anthropic's Alignment Science team. He continues foundational work on probabilistic and generative modeling while studying how to evaluate and govern increasingly capable AI and mitigate catastrophic risks from future systems.

faculty.name: Roger Grosse
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Grosse studies deep learning theory, optimization, and AI safety. He pioneered scalable influence-function methods that trace which training examples shape a large language model's behavior, advancing interpretability and alignment. A Vector Institute founding member and Schmidt AI2050 Fellow, he also works with Anthropic's Alignment Science team on training-data attribution. Recent work studies LLM generalization, data complexity via Boltzmann influence functions, and second-order optimization, using an understanding of deep learning to improve the safety of AI systems.

faculty.name: Sanja Fidler
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Fidler leads NVIDIA's AI research lab in Toronto and co-founded the Vector Institute, where she is a Canada CIFAR AI Chair. Her group works on 3D computer vision, generative models for 3D content, neural rendering, world models, and interactive annotation. Recent work spans large-scale 3D and text-to-3D generation, differentiable simulation, and perception for autonomous driving and robotics. She bridges academic vision research with industrial-scale generative 3D and spatial-intelligence systems.

faculty.name: Sheila McIlraith
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: McIlraith works at the intersection of automated planning, knowledge representation, and reinforcement learning. Her group introduced reward machines and pioneered using linear temporal logic and automata to specify non-Markovian rewards and guide RL agents. A Canada CIFAR AI Chair and associate director of the Schwartz Reisman Institute, she also studies AI safety, sequential decision-making, and human-aligned objective specification. Recent papers advance LTL-guided exploration and formal task specification for reinforcement learning agents.

faculty.name: Gerald Penn
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Penn researches spoken language processing, computational linguistics, and the mathematical foundations of language. His joint work with Geoffrey Hinton and Hui Jiang on deep neural networks for acoustic modeling helped transform speech recognition and won an IEEE Signal Processing Society best-paper award. His group studies speech and prosody, mathematical linguistics, formal grammar, the theory of programming languages, and archaeological decipherment, combining rigorous formalism with statistical and neural approaches to natural language and spoken-language understanding.

faculty.name: Faith Ellen
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Ellen is a leading theorist of distributed computing, focusing on the complexity of concurrent and distributed data structures and proving lower bounds that reveal how model parameters affect computational power. Her work analyzes shared-memory algorithms, wait-free and lock-free data structures, and the fundamental limits of distributed coordination. A longtime Toronto professor and leader in the PODC research community, she continues to publish foundational results on concurrent data structures and the theory of distributed and sequential computation.

faculty.name: Bianca Schroeder
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Schroeder, a Canada Research Chair, studies the design, reliability, and performance of large-scale computer and storage systems. Her influential empirical studies of disk, DRAM, and flash failures in real data centers reshaped how the field models hardware reliability, earning multiple USENIX Test of Time awards. Recent work applies machine learning, statistics, and data mining to predict and prevent storage errors and to improve resource management, bridging large-scale systems measurement with data-driven reliability and efficiency techniques.

faculty.name: Daniel Wigdor
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Wigdor works in human-computer interaction, focusing on high-performance user-interface architectures, post-WIMP and touch and gesture interaction, and platforms for ubiquitous and mobile computing. Based in the Dynamic Graphics Project, he also directs research at Meta's Reality Labs Toronto and has co-founded HCI startups. His research develops new interaction techniques and the underlying software architectures that make responsive, expressive interfaces possible across mobile, tabletop, and mixed-reality devices, with a broad goal of using computing to help people live better.

faculty.name: Alec Jacobson
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Jacobson, a Canada Research Chair, is a leader in geometry processing and computer graphics. He created the widely used libigl geometry-processing library and works on shape deformation, robust mesh generation, geometric optimization, and computational fabrication. His recent research blends classical geometry processing with machine learning for 3D shape analysis, generation, and simulation. He is also a senior research scientist at Adobe Research and founded the Toronto Geometry Colloquium, a web series on geometry processing.

faculty.name: Chris Maddison
faculty.title: Assistant Professor, Department of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Maddison develops the methodology of statistical machine learning, emphasizing methods that scale in deep learning. A founding member of DeepMind's AlphaGo project, he is best known for gradient-estimation techniques such as the Concrete/Gumbel-softmax distribution that are now standard tools. His recent work spans Bayesian inference, optimization, discrete search, and sampling, with applications to protein and scientific discovery. Jointly appointed in Statistical Sciences and a CIFAR AI Chair at the Vector Institute, he aims to build learning algorithms that reason reliably in stubbornly complex settings.

faculty.name: Colin Raffel
faculty.title: Associate Professor, Department of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Raffel works to decentralize, democratize, and de-risk large-scale AI. He co-created the influential T5 model and the transfer-learning framework behind much of modern NLP, and now studies open large language models, model merging, collaborative and continual model development, and machine unlearning. Recent projects include data-centric training of small language models (SmolLM2) and building LLMs from openly licensed text to address IP and ethical concerns. An associate research director at the Vector Institute and faculty researcher at Hugging Face, he champions openly published, community-driven machine learning.

faculty.name: Gennady Pekhimenko
faculty.title: Associate Professor, Department of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Pekhimenko leads the EcoSystem research group, building computer systems that are faster, more energy-efficient, and robust. His work spans computer architecture, hardware acceleration, compilers, and systems for machine learning, including memory compression, efficient training and inference, and rigorous ML benchmarking through efforts like MLPerf. A Vector Institute faculty member and CIFAR AI Chair, he founded CentML to optimize deep-learning deployment. His research bridges architecture and ML to reduce the cost and energy of training and serving large models across the hardware-software stack.

faculty.name: Eyal de Lara
faculty.title: Professor and Chair, Department of Computer Science
faculty.summary: De Lara, Chair of the department, researches cloud, mobile, and edge computing. He has contributed novel algorithms for system virtualization, application scaling, indoor localization, mobile security, and continuous mobile sensing, and has helped define edge computing as a discipline through venues like ACM MobiSys and ACM/IEEE SEC. His group builds systems that push computation closer to users and devices for lower latency and better privacy, tackling how software should be partitioned and orchestrated across the growing continuum from cloud datacenters to the network edge.

faculty.name: Nisarg Shah
faculty.title: Associate Professor, Department of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Shah works at the intersection of computer science and economics, studying fairness, efficiency, incentives, and elicitation in algorithmic decision-making. He is known for foundational results in fair division and computational social choice, including analyses of maximum Nash welfare and envy-freeness for allocating indivisible resources. His work informs practical systems for dividing goods, aggregating preferences, and participatory budgeting. Recent research develops algorithmic notions of fairness for machine learning and multi-agent settings, aiming to give provable guarantees when automated decisions affect people.

faculty.name: Aleksandar Nikolov
faculty.title: Associate Professor, Department of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Nikolov is a theoretical computer scientist and Canada Research Chair in Algorithms and Private Data Analysis. He applies tools from discrepancy theory and high-dimensional geometry to differential privacy, designing algorithms that answer many statistical queries accurately while provably protecting individuals. His work spans experimental design, optimization, and the geometry of data, characterizing the fundamental accuracy limits of private analysis. Recent research studies factorization mechanisms and private query release, connecting rigorous mathematics with the practical question of how to responsibly analyze sensitive data.

faculty.name: Yang Xu
faculty.title: Associate Professor, Department of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Xu takes a computational approach to the cognitive foundations of language, directing the Cognitive Lexicon Laboratory. He studies how word meanings change over time and how people extend, combine, and innovate words to express emerging concepts, uniting natural language processing with cognitive science. His models predict semantic change, metaphor, and lexical innovation across languages and child language development. Jointly appointed in the Cognitive Science Program, he bridges NLP, cognitive modeling, and linguistics to explain how human vocabulary adapts efficiently to communicative needs.

faculty.name: David Fleet
faculty.title: Professor, Department of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Fleet researches computer vision and machine learning, focusing on latent-variable and generative models for images, video, and human pose and motion. His group advanced diffusion models for high-fidelity generation, and his work on Google's Imagen text-to-image system won a NeurIPS best-paper award. Earlier contributions to optical flow and motion estimation remain foundational. A Research Director with Google DeepMind in Toronto and a Vector Institute faculty member, he continues to develop probabilistic generative models that connect perception, synthesis, and structured representation of the visual world.

faculty.name: Tovi Grossman
faculty.title: Professor, Department of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Grossman is an HCI researcher in the Dynamic Graphics Project, inventing interaction techniques and studying how people learn to use complex software. His work spans input and interaction, augmented and virtual reality, digital fabrication, software learnability, and, increasingly, human-centered applications of AI. He has published extensively at CHI and UIST, earning best-paper recognition and lasting influence on tool design. Co-founder and chief scientist at AXL Labs, he explores how intuitive interfaces and generative AI can be combined to make powerful creative tools broadly accessible.

faculty.name: Eitan Grinspun
faculty.title: Professor, Department of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Grinspun researches computer graphics and scientific computing, using geometry as the bridge between physical and computational principles. He is a leader in discrete differential geometry, physical simulation, and contact mechanics, developing algorithms that realistically animate cloth, hair, rods, and elastic materials for film, engineering, and medicine. Recent work integrates neural methods with simulation, including neural cloth super-resolution and self-collision handling. A member of the Dynamic Graphics Project, his widely used simulation techniques have been adopted across visual effects and computational design.

faculty.name: Anna Goldenberg
faculty.title: Professor, Department of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Goldenberg develops machine-learning methods to decipher human disease heterogeneity by integrating diverse biomedical data. Her lab builds models for patient similarity, multi-omics integration, and clinical prediction, and quantifies variability in health across individuals and populations using wearables. She holds the Varma Family Chair in Medical Bioinformatics and AI at SickKids, where she co-leads the AI in Medicine for Kids initiative to bring machine learning safely into pediatric care. Her work spans computational biology, health informatics, and trustworthy clinical deployment of AI, with a Vector Institute affiliation.

faculty.name: Anwar Hithnawi
faculty.title: Assistant Professor, Department of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Hithnawi leads the Privacy Preserving Systems Lab, building systems that protect data and privacy without sacrificing functionality or performance. Her research combines applied cryptography and systems to secure data not just at rest and in transit but while in use, making techniques like homomorphic encryption, secure multi-party computation, and zero-knowledge proofs practical and deployable. She works on privacy-preserving machine learning, federated systems, and verifiable computation. Previously at ETH Zurich, she designs infrastructure that lets organizations compute over sensitive data with strong, provable privacy guarantees.

requirements.english: No language test required; strong written and spoken English expected (submit proof only if available).
requirements.admission_model_note: Applicants must secure a supervising professor before admission. You can contact professors directly about openings, or submit a central online application that is automatically made available to and reviewed by the entire D-INFK faculty; shortlisted candidates are typically interviewed.

funding.note: Doctoral students are salaried ETH employees, hired by their professor and paid as research/teaching assistants (D-INFK salary rates 4-5), which comfortably covers living costs in Zürich. Typical duration is 4-5 years.

requirements.contact_note: Contacting professors directly to ask about doctoral openings is a standard and encouraged first step, since admission requires finding a supervisor.

faculty.name: Gustavo Alonso
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Alonso leads the Systems Group, working at the intersection of data processing and modern hardware. Recent work focuses on hardware acceleration of databases and machine learning using FPGAs and smart NICs, hardware-software co-design, disaggregated and distributed data centers, and near-data processing. His group builds systems that exploit specialized hardware to accelerate analytics, transactions, and ML inference, bridging computer architecture, databases, and distributed systems for next-generation cloud infrastructure.

faculty.name: Torsten Hoefler
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Hoefler directs the Scalable Parallel Computing Lab, focusing on high-performance computing and large-scale systems for AI. Recent work spans efficient training and inference of large language models, sparse and low-precision deep learning, network topology and communication optimization, MPI standards, and performance modeling for supercomputers. His group develops the software and networking infrastructure that enables scaling deep learning to thousands of accelerators, and he is a leading voice in HPC-for-AI research.

faculty.name: David Basin
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Basin leads the Information Security Group, applying formal methods to real-world security. His group develops the Tamarin prover for automated security-protocol verification and has produced machine-checked analyses of TLS 1.3, 5G authentication, and EMV payment protocols. Recent work covers model-driven security, runtime monitoring and enforcement, privacy, and verified cryptographic protocol design. He bridges rigorous logic-based verification with practical impact on widely deployed communication and payment standards.

faculty.name: Andreas Krause
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Krause leads the Learning and Adaptive Systems Group and chairs the ETH AI Center. His research covers probabilistic machine learning, Bayesian optimization, bandits, active learning, and safe and data-efficient reinforcement learning, with theoretical guarantees and applications across the natural sciences and robotics. Recent work includes safe exploration, foundation models for scientific discovery, and uncertainty-aware decision making. He is one of Europe's most cited machine-learning researchers and a leader in trustworthy, sample-efficient learning.

faculty.name: Thomas Hofmann
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Hofmann leads the Data Analytics Lab, working on deep learning theory and practice. Recent research covers large language models, transformer architectures and their optimization, representation learning, generative models, and the science of training dynamics and loss landscapes. His group studies both foundational questions about why deep networks generalize and applied problems in natural language understanding and information retrieval. He previously held senior roles in industry research before returning to academia.

faculty.name: Martin Vechev
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Vechev leads the Secure, Reliable, and Intelligent Systems (SRI) Lab, combining machine learning, program analysis, and security. His group pioneered certified robustness and formal verification of neural networks, AI for code and program synthesis, differential privacy, and safe and trustworthy AI systems. Recent work addresses robustness and safety of large language models, privacy attacks and defenses, and neuro-symbolic reasoning. He also co-founded companies commercializing AI-driven code and security tools built on this research.

faculty.name: Marc Pollefeys
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Pollefeys leads the Computer Vision and Geometry Group, specializing in 3D scene understanding. His research covers structure-from-motion, dense 3D reconstruction, simultaneous localization and mapping, visual and semantic localization, and learning-based geometric perception for robotics and augmented reality. Recent work integrates deep learning with multi-view geometry for robust real-time mapping and scene understanding. He has also directed a large mixed-reality and AI research lab at Microsoft, connecting his academic work to widely deployed spatial-computing systems.

faculty.name: Olga Sorkine-Hornung
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Sorkine-Hornung leads the Interactive Geometry Lab, focusing on digital geometry processing and interactive content creation. Her research covers shape modeling and deformation, mesh processing, differential-geometry-based algorithms, physically based simulation, and computational design for digital fabrication. Recent work develops intuitive tools for artists and engineers to create, edit, and manufacture 3D shapes, as well as learning-based geometry methods. She is a widely recognized leader in computer graphics and a recipient of major early- and mid-career research awards.

faculty.name: Angelika Steger
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Steger leads the Combinatorial Structures and Algorithms group, working in discrete mathematics and theoretical computer science. Her research centers on random graphs and structures, probabilistic and extremal combinatorics, randomized algorithms, and the analysis of random processes. Recent work also explores theoretical models of neural computation and learning. She is a leading figure in European theoretical computer science, known for deep results at the interface of probability, combinatorics, and algorithm design.

faculty.name: Ryan Cotterell
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Cotterell leads the Rycolab group, working on natural language processing and computational linguistics with a strong theoretical bent. His research applies information theory, formal language theory, and statistics to study language models, morphology, phonology, and multilingual NLP. Recent work analyzes the expressivity and mechanics of transformers and language models, interpretability, and rigorous evaluation. He is a prolific and influential young researcher who emphasizes mathematically principled approaches to understanding both human language and modern NLP systems.

faculty.name: Timothy Roscoe
faculty.title: Full Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Timothy "Mothy" Roscoe is a Full Professor in the Systems Group at ETH Zürich, where he has also served as head of department. His research spans operating systems, distributed systems, and computer networks, with a strong emphasis on the hardware-software interface. He leads work on the Enzian research computer, a custom platform for exploring datacenter and heterogeneous system architectures. His interests include rethinking OS structure for modern multicore and specialized hardware, enterprise computing, and building robust, high-performance systems software from the ground up.

faculty.name: Ana Klimovic
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Ana Klimovic is an Assistant Professor of Computer Science at ETH Zürich, where she leads the Efficient Architectures and Systems Lab within the Systems Group. Her research designs computer systems for large-scale cloud applications, data analytics, and machine learning, aiming to improve performance and resource efficiency. She works across operating systems, distributed systems, storage, and serverless computing, and their intersection with hardware and ML. Before ETH, she completed a PhD at Stanford and spent a year as a research scientist at Google Brain.

faculty.name: Srdjan Capkun
faculty.title: Full Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Srdjan Capkun is a Full Professor of Computer Science at ETH Zürich, where he leads the System Security Group and chairs the Zurich Information Security and Privacy Center. His research focuses on system and network security, including wireless and physical-layer security, secure positioning and localization, trusted computing, and the security of distributed ledgers. He investigates how to design and verify systems that remain secure against powerful adversaries. He earned his PhD in communication systems from EPFL before joining ETH Zürich.

faculty.name: Adrian Perrig
faculty.title: Full Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Adrian Perrig is a Full Professor of Computer Science at ETH Zürich, where he leads the Network Security Group. His research centers on building secure and robust network systems, most notably the SCION next-generation Internet architecture, which he co-designed to provide high availability and security by design. His broader interests include networking, systems security, and internet infrastructure. Before returning to ETH, he was a professor at Carnegie Mellon University. He is widely recognized for foundational contributions to secure protocols and network architecture.

faculty.name: Kenny Paterson
faculty.title: Full Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Kenneth (Kenny) Paterson is a Full Professor in the Institute of Information Security at ETH Zürich, where he leads the Applied Cryptography Group. His research bridges cryptographic theory and real-world practice, analyzing and improving the security of deployed protocols and systems such as TLS, secure messaging, and encrypted databases. He works on provable security, cryptographic protocol design, and cryptography in the wild. Formerly at Royal Holloway, University of London, he is an IACR Fellow and Editor-in-Chief of the Journal of Cryptology.

faculty.name: Florian Tramèr
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Florian Tramèr is an Assistant Professor of Computer Science at ETH Zürich, where he leads the SPY Lab. His research sits at the intersection of machine learning, computer security, privacy, and cryptography, studying the worst-case, adversarial behavior of deep learning systems. He investigates data poisoning, model extraction, membership inference, and privacy leakage in modern ML pipelines, aiming to make AI systems trustworthy and safe. He completed his PhD at Stanford under Dan Boneh and spent a year at Google Brain.

faculty.name: Rasmus Kyng
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Rasmus Kyng is an Associate Professor of Computer Science at ETH Zürich, leading the Algorithms and Optimization Group. His research develops fast algorithms for graph problems and convex optimization, including breakthrough work on near-optimal maximum-flow and minimum-cost flow algorithms. He also works on dynamic graph data structures, numerical linear algebra, spectral methods, and random matrix theory, with applications to machine learning and scientific computing. His results have earned FOCS best-paper awards and the ETH Zürich Latsis Prize.

faculty.name: Fanny Yang
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Fanny Yang is an Associate Professor of Computer Science at ETH Zürich, where she leads the Statistical Machine Learning Group. Her research investigates the theoretical foundations of modern machine learning, with a focus on reliability, robustness, and fairness. She studies generalization in overparameterized models, high-dimensional and nonparametric statistics, distributional robustness, domain generalization, and interpretability. She earned her PhD at UC Berkeley with Martin Wainwright and held postdoctoral positions at Stanford before joining ETH Zürich, where she was tenured in 2026.

faculty.name: Markus Gross
faculty.title: Full Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Markus Gross is a Full Professor of Computer Science at ETH Zürich and head of the Computer Graphics Laboratory, which he founded in 1994. He is also a director of Disney Research and a former Chief Scientist at Walt Disney Studios. His research covers computer graphics, geometric modeling, physically based animation, image and video processing, display technology, and digital fabrication. Author of hundreds of papers and many patents, he has shaped both academic graphics research and its application in film and entertainment.

faculty.name: Siyu Tang
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Siyu Tang is an Assistant Professor of Computer Science at ETH Zürich, where she leads the Computer Vision and Learning Group in the Institute of Visual Computing. Her research focuses on computer vision and machine learning for perceiving and modeling humans, developing computational models that reconstruct human motion, bodies, and activities from visual input. She works on 3D human reconstruction, virtual humans, motion tracking, and neural scene representations. Her work bridges perception, graphics, and learning to capture people and their interactions with environments.

faculty.name: Mrinmaya Sachan
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Mrinmaya Sachan is a Tenure-Track Assistant Professor of Computer Science at ETH Zürich, working on natural language processing and machine learning. His research spans language understanding, knowledge representation and reasoning, and multimodal learning, with a strong interest in AI for education and intelligent tutoring systems. He develops models that reason over text and structured knowledge and that support personalized learning. He earned his PhD at Carnegie Mellon University and received an ACL Outstanding Paper Award along with several industry fellowships during his doctoral studies.

faculty.name: Peter Müller
faculty.title: Full Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Peter Müller is a Full Professor of Computer Science at ETH Zürich, where he leads the Programming Methodology Group. His research focuses on the construction and analysis of reliable software through program verification, static analysis, type systems, and automated test generation. His group develops the Viper verification infrastructure, which underpins deductive verifiers for languages such as Rust, Go, and Python using separation logic. He works on making formal reasoning about complex, concurrent programs practical and scalable for real-world software.

faculty.name: Christian Holz
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Christian Holz is an Associate Professor of Computer Science at ETH Zürich, where he leads the Sensing, Interaction and Perception Lab. His research spans human-computer interaction, mixed and virtual reality, wearable and ubiquitous computing, and mobile health sensing. He develops novel input techniques, sensing systems, and signal-processing methods for interaction in immersive settings, as well as continuous health monitoring for predictive diagnostics and personalized medicine. His work combines hardware prototyping, sensing, and machine learning to connect people, devices, and their physiological signals.

faculty.name: Yannis Chronis
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Yannis Chronis is a Tenure-Track Assistant Professor of Computer Science at ETH Zürich and a member of the Systems Group, with an associated appointment at the ETH AI Center. His research is anchored in data management, spanning query optimization, indexing, physical database design, and data management for machine learning and AI workloads. He works on hardware-software co-design to make data access efficient and database systems robust and easy to use. He was previously affiliated with Google before joining ETH Zürich in 2025.

faculty.name: Yuvraj Agarwal
faculty.title: Associate Professor

faculty.name: Fei Fang

faculty.name: Bogdan Vasilescu
faculty.title: Associate Professor

faculty.name: Hoda Heidari
faculty.title: Assistant Professor (Joint with MLD)

faculty.name: Steven Wu
faculty.title: Assistant Professor

faculty.name: Andrew Ilyas
faculty.title: Assistant Professor

faculty.name: Sarah Scheffler
faculty.title: Assistant Professor

faculty.name: Riccardo Paccagnella
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Research on cybersecurity and systems security including hardware security, side-channel attacks and defenses, and secure computer architecture.

faculty.name: Richard Zemel

faculty.name: Elias Bareinboim

faculty.name: Vladimir Vapnik

faculty.name: Daniel Hsu
faculty.title: Associate Professor

faculty.name: David Knowles
faculty.title: Associate Professor

faculty.name: Rocco Servedio
faculty.title: Professor

faculty.name: Julia Hirschberg
faculty.title: Percy K. and Vida L. W. Hudson Professor

faculty.name: Kathleen McKeown
faculty.title: Henry and Gertrude Rothschilds Professor

faculty.name: Zhou Yu
faculty.title: Associate Professor

faculty.name: John Hewitt

faculty.name: Shih-Fu Chang
faculty.title: Richard Dicker Professor of Electrical Engineering (Joint with CS)

faculty.name: Shree Nayar
faculty.title: T. C. Chang Professor

faculty.name: Carl Vondrick
faculty.title: YM Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Research on computer vision, machine learning, robotics, and perception, with focus on video understanding and visual reasoning.

faculty.name: Peter Belhumeur
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Research on computer vision, graphics, and face recognition. Pioneer in facial recognition technologies.

faculty.name: Yunzhu Li
faculty.title: Assistant Professor

faculty.name: Tal Malkin
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Research on cryptography, complexity theory, security, and randomized algorithms.

faculty.name: Salvatore Stolfo
faculty.title: Morysa Engineering Professor
faculty.summary: Research on computer security, intrusion detection, embedded device security, and data mining/machine learning for security.

faculty.name: Suman Jana
faculty.title: Associate Professor

faculty.name: Roxana Geambasu
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Research on distributed systems, operating systems, security and privacy, cloud computing, and mobile computing.

faculty.name: Junfeng Yang
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Research on operating systems, programming languages, security, and distributed systems, with focus on building reliable and secure software.

faculty.name: Simha Sethumadhavan
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Research on computer architecture, security, VLSI design, and high-performance computing with focus on hardware security.

faculty.name: Jeannette Wing
faculty.title: Professor; EVP for Research
faculty.summary: Research on trustworthy AI, security and privacy, probabilistic programming, and formal methods. Former Corporate VP at Microsoft Research.

faculty.name: Tim Roughgarden
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Research on algorithms, algorithmic game theory, mechanism design, and blockchains. Leading theorist and award-winning educator.

faculty.name: Noémie Elhadad
faculty.title: Associate Professor; Department Chair
faculty.summary: Research at the intersection of AI, human-centered computing, and medicine. Develops ML methods and AI tools for patients and clinicians. Leads eve_n initiative on data-powered women's health.

faculty.name: George Hripcsak
faculty.title: Vivian Beaumont Allen Professor of Biomedical Informatics
faculty.summary: Pioneer in biomedical informatics, former DBMI chair for 15 years. Research on observational health research, causal inference in healthcare AI, and clinical data science.

faculty.name: Chunhua Weng
faculty.title: Professor of Biomedical Informatics (in Medicine)
faculty.summary: Research on clinical research informatics, NLP for clinical trial eligibility criteria, and translational informatics for patient-centered care.

faculty.name: Andrea Califano
faculty.title: Clyde '56 and Helen Wu Professor of Chemical Biology (in Systems Biology); Professor of Biomedical Informatics
faculty.summary: Research on systems biology and cancer genomics. Leads the Califano Lab at the Institute for Cancer Genomics.

faculty.name: Raul Rabadan
faculty.title: Professor of Systems Biology and Biomedical Informatics
faculty.summary: Research on systems biology and biomedical informatics, with focus on cancer genomics and computational methods.

faculty.name: Lena Mamykina
faculty.title: Associate Professor; Graduate Program Director
faculty.summary: Research on human-computer interaction and health informatics, with focus on self-management and patient-centered design for chronic disease. Serves as Graduate Program Director.

faculty.name: Shalmali Joshi
faculty.title: Assistant Professor (DBMI and CS)
faculty.summary: Research on algorithmic safety of machine learning for healthcare. Develops causal inference, probabilistic modeling, and deep learning methods. Leads the reAIM Lab. Affiliated with CS.

faculty.name: Matthew McDermott
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Research on machine learning and clinical AI for healthcare applications.

faculty.name: Xuhai Orson Xu
faculty.title: Assistant Professor (DBMI, CS, Data Science Institute)
faculty.summary: Research on HCI and AI for health. Leads the SEA Lab. Joint appointment with CS and Data Science Institute. Recruiting PhD students for Fall 2026.

faculty.name: Despina Kontos
faculty.title: Herbert and Florence Irving Professor of Radiological Sciences; Professor of Biomedical Informatics
faculty.summary: Research on AI in medical imaging, radiomics, and machine learning for cancer imaging and precision medicine.

faculty.name: Suzanne Bakken
faculty.title: Alumni Professor of Nursing; Professor of Biomedical Informatics
faculty.summary: Research on nursing informatics, health equity, precision health, and community health informatics.

faculty.name: Yufeng Shen
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Systems Biology and Biomedical Informatics
faculty.summary: Research on systems biology and biomedical informatics, with focus on genomics and statistical genetics.

faculty.name: Dennis Vitkup
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Systems Biology and Biomedical Informatics
faculty.summary: Research on systems biology and computational biology, with focus on metabolic networks and genomics.

faculty.name: Aristidis Floratos
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Systems Biology and Biomedical Informatics
faculty.summary: Research on systems biology and biomedical informatics with focus on computational genomics.

faculty.name: Sarah Rossetti
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Biomedical Informatics and Nursing
faculty.summary: Research on clinical informatics, patient safety, and health IT.

faculty.name: Lorenzo Alvisi
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Tisch University Professor and Chair of Computer Science. Research focuses on distributed systems, fault tolerance, and blockchain technologies, with emphasis on building reliable systems from unreliable components.

faculty.name: Yoav Artzi
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Associate Professor at Cornell Tech. Works on natural language processing with a focus on language grounding in situated contexts, semantic parsing, and learning through interaction.

faculty.name: Tapomayukh Bhattacharjee
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Leads the EmPRISE Lab focusing on robot manipulation and human-robot interaction for assistive care. Develops algorithms for robots to physically interact with people and objects in unstructured environments.

faculty.name: David Bindel
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Works on numerical analysis, scientific computing, and high-performance computing. Develops algorithms and software for large-scale eigenvalue problems, inverse problems, and computational science applications.

faculty.name: Ken Birman
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: N. Rama Rao Professor of Computer Science. Pioneer in reliable distributed computing, known for the Isis Toolkit and virtual synchrony model. Current work spans cloud computing, IoT, and machine learning at the edge.

faculty.name: Eshan Chattopadhyay
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Works in theoretical computer science, focusing on pseudorandomness, derandomization, randomness extraction, and explicit constructions of combinatorial objects. Interested in the power and limits of randomness in computation.

faculty.name: Christopher De Sa
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Develops novel algorithms and systems for large-scale machine learning, focusing on optimization methods and approximate inference. Works on making ML training more efficient and theoretically sound.

faculty.name: Sarah Dean
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Works at the intersection of machine learning and control theory. Develops algorithms for safe and robust decision-making in dynamical systems, with applications to robotics and autonomous systems.

faculty.name: Kevin Ellis
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Works on program synthesis and neurosymbolic AI, building systems that learn to write code from examples and natural language. Combines techniques from machine learning, programming languages, and cognitive science.

faculty.name: Bharath Hariharan
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Works on computer vision and machine learning, with interests in few-shot learning, visual recognition, and compositional visual reasoning. Develops techniques for learning from limited labeled data.

faculty.name: Joseph Halpern
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Joseph C. Ford Chair of Engineering. Leading researcher in reasoning about knowledge, uncertainty, and causality. Contributions span AI, philosophy of computation, game theory, and distributed computing theory.

faculty.name: Thorsten Joachims
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Jacob Gould Schurman Professor of Computer Science and Information Science. Director of the Cornell AI Initiative. Pioneering work on support vector machines, learning to rank, and interactive machine learning systems that learn from implicit feedback.

faculty.name: Ari Juels
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Weill Family Foundation Professor at Cornell Tech. Research spans computer security, cryptography, and blockchain technologies. Known for work on proof-of-work, authenticated data structures, and secure smart contracts.

faculty.name: Jon Kleinberg
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Tisch University Professor of Computer Science and Information Science. Foundational contributions to network science, social network analysis (HITS algorithm), and algorithmic fairness. Works at the intersection of CS, economics, and sociology.

faculty.name: Lillian Lee
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Charles Roy Davis Professor of Computer Science. Leading researcher in NLP, particularly known for pioneering work in sentiment analysis and opinion mining. Also studies discourse, persuasion, and the relationship between language and human cognition.

faculty.name: Greg Morrisett
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Jack and Rilla Neafsey Dean and Vice Provost of Cornell Tech. Research on programming languages, compilers, formal verification, and software security. Known for work on typed assembly language, proof-carrying code, and verified systems software.

faculty.name: Andrew Myers
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Class of 1912 Professor of Engineering. Director of Graduate Studies. Develops programming language techniques for building secure and reliable systems, including language-based information flow control and secure compilation.

faculty.name: Rafael Pass
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Works on cryptography and theoretical computer science, with contributions to computational complexity, game-theoretic foundations of cryptography, and the theoretical underpinnings of blockchain protocols.

faculty.name: Adrian Sampson
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Works at the intersection of computer architecture and programming languages, with interests in approximate computing, reconfigurable hardware, and domain-specific languages for emerging hardware platforms.

faculty.name: Vitaly Shmatikov
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Research focuses on computer security and privacy, with significant recent work on security and privacy of machine learning systems, including adversarial examples, model extraction attacks, and privacy-preserving ML.

faculty.name: Noah Snavely
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Leading researcher in computer vision and computer graphics. Known for pioneering work on large-scale 3D reconstruction from internet photo collections, image-based rendering, and scene understanding.

faculty.name: Karthik Sridharan
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Works on the theoretical foundations of machine learning, including online learning, statistical learning theory, and connections between learning theory and optimization. Also studies algorithmic fairness.

faculty.name: Éva Tardos
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Jacob Gould Schurman Professor of Computer Science. World-renowned for foundational contributions to combinatorial optimization, approximation algorithms, and algorithmic game theory. Studies strategic behavior in networks and markets.

faculty.name: Kilian Weinberger
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Research spans machine learning, deep learning, and their applications. Known for work on distance metric learning, deep learning model compression for edge devices, and robust machine learning.

faculty.name: Rachit Agarwal
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Works on designing and building practical systems across the computing stack, from computer architecture to distributed systems and networking, with a focus on datacenter-scale computing.

funding.note: Fully funded (tuition, stipend, health insurance) for PhD students

faculty.name: Shiri Azenkot
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Leads the Enhancing Ability Lab at Cornell Tech. Designs, builds, and evaluates accessible and emerging technologies for people with disabilities, with focus on visual accessibility, AR/VR accessibility, and autonomous vehicle interaction.

faculty.name: Cristobal Cheyre
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Research lies at the intersection of information science and economics, studying how digital information systems shape economic behavior, privacy decisions, and the regulatory landscape of AI and data.

faculty.name: Chris Csikszentmihalyi
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Works on critical and humanistic approaches to computing, blending art, technology, and social action. Research focuses on community-based technology design, environmental computing, and the social implications of digital systems.

faculty.name: Cristian Danescu-Niculescu-Mizil
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Develops computational frameworks for understanding social and conversational dynamics in online communities. Known for work on linguistic style matching, politeness in online conversations, and conversational behavior change over time.

faculty.name: Nicola Dell
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Leads the Interface Design and Development Lab at Cornell Tech. Designs, builds, and evaluates technologies to improve the lives of underserved populations, with projects spanning global health, digital safety, and accessible computing.

faculty.name: Susan Fussell
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Professor of Information Science and Communications, Director of Graduate Studies. Studies computer-mediated communication and computer-supported cooperative work, with interests in remote collaboration, social media, and cross-cultural communication in teams.

faculty.name: François Guimbretière
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Designs and builds novel interactive systems spanning digital fabrication, pen-based interfaces, and augmented reality. Develops new hardware and software tools that push the boundaries of how humans interact with digital information.

faculty.name: Steve Jackson
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Vice Provost for Academic Innovation. Research examines how infrastructure, technology, and governance shape social and political life. Known for work on repair, maintenance, and the ethics of large-scale sociotechnical systems.

faculty.name: Thorsten Joachims
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Jacob Gould Schurman Professor. Director of the Cornell AI Initiative. Pioneering work on support vector machines for ranking, learning from implicit feedback signals, and interactive machine learning with human judgments.

faculty.name: Wendy Ju
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies human-robot interaction and interaction design for autonomous systems, including autonomous vehicles. Research focuses on how people interact with and understand intelligent, embodied technologies in everyday contexts.

faculty.name: Malte Jung
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Nancy H. '62 and Philip M. '62 Young Sesquicentennial Faculty Fellow. Studies how robots and AI systems shape team dynamics, emotion, and collaboration. Designs robots that can effectively participate in human social groups and teamwork.

faculty.name: Rene Kizilcec
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies the design, deployment, and evaluation of digital learning environments at scale. Research examines how technology can reduce or amplify educational inequalities, with focus on MOOCs, behavioral interventions, and algorithmic fairness in education.

faculty.name: Jon Kleinberg
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Tisch University Professor of Computer Science and Information Science. Foundational contributions to network science (HITS algorithm) and algorithmic fairness. Studies the structure of social and information networks and the societal implications of algorithms.

faculty.name: Karen Levy
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies the intersection of technology, law, and society, with particular focus on surveillance, privacy, and the regulation of data-driven systems. Author of Data Driven: Truckers, Technology, and the New Workplace Surveillance.

faculty.name: David Mimno
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Chair of Information Science. Develops statistical methods for analyzing large text collections, with contributions to topic modeling and computational approaches to the humanities and social sciences. Studies bias and reproducibility in text analysis.

faculty.name: Mor Naaman
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Don and Mibs Follett Professor at Cornell Tech. Associate Dean for Faculty Affairs. Studies how information spreads and is consumed on social media, with work on misinformation, trust in digital platforms, and computational approaches to media analysis.

faculty.name: Helen Nissenbaum
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Andrew H. and Ann R. Tisch Professor. World-renowned privacy scholar known for developing the theory of contextual integrity, which has become a foundational framework for understanding and regulating privacy in the digital age.

faculty.name: Tapan Parikh
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Designs and builds information systems for underserved and marginalized communities globally. Work spans mobile data collection tools, community media platforms, and participatory design methods for international development contexts.

faculty.name: Phoebe Sengers
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Integrates critical theory and cultural analysis with human-computer interaction research. Known for pioneering work in reflective design, rural computing, and examining the cultural assumptions embedded in technology design practices.

faculty.name: Daniel Susser
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Works on the ethics, law, and policy of AI and digital technology. Examines algorithmic fairness and discrimination, privacy, and governance frameworks for emerging technologies, bridging philosophy, law, and computer science.

faculty.name: Matthew Wilkens
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Associate Dean for Education. Applies computational methods, particularly NLP and machine learning, to large-scale analysis of literature and culture. Studies literary history, cultural trends, and novel detection at scale.

faculty.name: Cheng Zhang
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Leads the Smart Computer Interfaces for Future Interactions (SciFi) Lab. Develops intelligent sensing systems and wearable devices for health monitoring, human activity recognition, and novel human-computer interaction methods.

faculty.name: Aditya Vashistha
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Builds and audits AI systems for marginalized communities globally. Research spans accessibility, ICT4D, AI accountability, and human-centered NLP, with focus on ensuring AI technologies serve diverse and underserved populations.

faculty.name: Angelina Wang
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Studies algorithmic fairness, accountability, and the societal impact of machine learning. Audits AI systems for disparate impacts and develops technical and policy approaches to make AI systems more equitable and transparent.

faculty.name: Qian Yang
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Designs and studies interactive systems that enable people to effectively work with AI. Focuses on collaboration between humans and machine learning models, with work spanning explainable AI, human-in-the-loop systems, and design tools.

faculty.name: Devin J. Balkcom
faculty.title: Professor of Computer Science; Department Chair
faculty.summary: Research in robotics, optimal trajectories, robotic manipulation of flexible materials, and motion planning for knot-tying and underwater assembly.

faculty.name: Andrew T. Campbell
faculty.title: Albert Bradley 1915 Third Century Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in ubiquitous computing using embedded sensors and ML on phones and wearables to infer human behavior with applications to mental health. Leads the StudentLife project.

faculty.name: David F. Kotz
faculty.title: Pat and John Rosenwald Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in security and privacy in smart home environments, mobile health platforms, and wearable sensing. ACM, IEEE, and AAAS Fellow.

faculty.name: Soroush Vosoughi
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in NLP, machine learning, LLM alignment, and computational social science, focusing on misinformation detection and propagation.

faculty.name: Yu-Wing Tai
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in computer vision and generative AI including image/video editing, 3D reconstruction, and neural radiance fields. Senior Member IEEE.

faculty.name: Amit Chakrabarti
faculty.title: Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in theoretical computer science, communication complexity, data stream algorithms, and information theory.

faculty.name: Deeparnab Chakrabarty
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in algorithms, discrete optimization, sublinear algorithms, and algorithmic economics.

faculty.name: Prabhanjan (Prasad) Jayanti
faculty.title: James Frank Family Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in concurrent algorithms, distributed computing, synchronization, and fault tolerance.

faculty.name: Peter Winkler
faculty.title: William Morrill Professor of Mathematics and Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in discrete mathematics, theory of computing, probability, and combinatorics.

faculty.name: Wojciech Jarosz
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in computer graphics, physically based rendering, appearance modeling, and light transport simulation.

faculty.name: Yaoqing Yang
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in ML and deep learning theory, model robustness, 3D vision, LLM pruning, and distributed computing.

faculty.name: Sarah M. Preum
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in NLP, human-AI interaction, multimodal AI, and computational health, with focus on clinical communication.

faculty.name: Sean W. Smith
faculty.title: Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in security and privacy, trusted computing, IoT security, SCADA systems, and human factors in security.

faculty.name: Sergey Bratus
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science; Dartmouth Distinguished Professor in Cybersecurity
faculty.summary: Research in cybersecurity, software exploitation, weird machines, and Linux kernel security. Former DARPA PM.

faculty.name: Tam Vu
faculty.title: Thomas A. and Georgina Tugwell Russo 1977 Distinguished Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in mobile and networked systems, earable computing, physiological sensing, and cyber-physical systems security.

faculty.name: Alberto Quattrini Li
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in autonomous mobile robotics, multi-robot systems, aquatic/underwater robots, and SLAM.

faculty.name: Daniel Rockmore
faculty.title: William H. Neukom 1964 Distinguished Professor of Mathematics and Computer Science
faculty.summary: Director of the Neukom Institute. Research in complex systems, network analysis, machine learning, and computational harmonic analysis.

faculty.name: Souyoung Jin
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in computer vision, video understanding, multimodal AI, and privacy-preserving machine learning.

faculty.name: Kyungtae Kim
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Research in systems and software security, fuzzing, vulnerability discovery, and kernel security.

faculty.name: Nikhil Singh
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science; Co-Director of Digital Arts
faculty.summary: Research in human-AI interaction, creative AI, music and audio AI, agentic AI, and healthcare AI.

funding.note: Fully funded 5 yrs (stipend, tuition, fees, health insurance)

faculty.name: Cynthia D. Rudin
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Directs the Interpretable Machine Learning Lab, developing predictive models that are inherently interpretable rather than black-box. Pioneered the Rashomon set paradigm and optimal risk scores (RiskSLIM, FasterRisk) applied to healthcare, criminal justice, and energy reliability.

faculty.name: Ronald Parr
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Researches reinforcement learning and sequential decision-making under uncertainty, focusing on approximate dynamic programming, feature discovery for value function approximation, and connections between model simplicity and noise in machine learning.

faculty.name: Rong Ge
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies the optimization landscape of non-convex problems to explain why gradient-based methods succeed in training neural networks. Known for proving that matrix completion has no spurious local minima and developing simple variance-reduction algorithms for non-convex optimization.

faculty.name: Bhuwan Dhingra
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Leads DukeNLP lab, investigating how language models acquire, store, and communicate knowledge reliably. Works on retrieval-free knowledge attribution in pretraining data, calibration of long-form LLM generation, and building epistemic interfaces for trustworthy AI.

faculty.name: Jian Pei
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Department Chair and world-leading data mining researcher. Co-creator of the foundational FP-growth and PrefixSpan algorithms. Current research spans data and AI markets, fair and equitable data science, and interdisciplinary applications in healthcare and social networks.

faculty.name: Jun Yang
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Researches databases and data-intensive computing, including robust query optimization, computational journalism tools for automated fact-checking, and educational tools that help thousands of students learn SQL debugging through database theory.

faculty.name: Sudeepa Roy
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Works at the intersection of data management, causal inference, and database theory. Co-directs the AME Lab developing interpretable, scalable matching for causal analysis using SQL. Builds automated tools for debugging relational queries in database education.

faculty.name: Carlo Tomasi
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Leading computer vision researcher. Co-developed the KLT feature tracker, factorization methods for structure from motion, the Earth Mover's Distance image metric, and bilateral filtering. Current work includes optical flow for autonomous driving and medical image analysis.

faculty.name: Jeffrey S. Chase
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Leads the Network and Internet Computing Lab, focusing on operating systems, cloud infrastructure, and trust management. Pioneered green data center power management and developed accountable resource delegation frameworks for federated cloud utilities.

faculty.name: Danyang Zhuo
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Member of Duke Systems Group. Researches datacenter systems and networking, including RDMA performance isolation, managed RPC architectures, and ML systems for efficient LLM training and serving. Work published at SIGCOMM, NSDI, OSDI, and SOSP.

faculty.name: Bruce Maggs
faculty.title: Professor
